scenarios.scenario: A plain `admin` (not super_admin) calls GET /invites, POST /invites/:id/revoke, POST /invites/:id/resend, or POST /api/admin/users/:id/role with role='customer' against a staff member.
scenarios.rationale: The plan's Public Contracts for all 3 new routes place the inline `req.adminSession.role !== 'super_admin' -> 403` check FIRST, before any other guard — the same pattern already proven at packages/api/src/routes/admin/staff.ts:211-213 (POST /invite) and packages/api/src/routes/admin/users.ts:57-59 (POST /users/:id/role, which Part B reuses byte-identical). This is a design-conformance check, not yet a running test — EXECUTE must implement the guard in that exact position for the 3 new routes; the plan does not deviate from the established pattern anywhere in its Public Contracts section.

scenarios.scenario: The plan's design does NOT actually apply the new revoked_at guard at every liveness-check site — some site keeps admitting a revoked token, silently defeating the entire feature (the SPEC's own headline correctness invariant).
scenarios.rationale: Enumerated all 4 known 'is this invite live' sites and cross-checked each against the plan text: (1) staff-invite.ts /start guard (line 68, currently 2-arm OR) — plan Section B step 4 adds a 3rd OR-arm for revokedAt !== null, confirmed against the live 2-arm condition; (2) staff-invite.ts /consume atomic WHERE (lines 153-159, currently 4 and()-ed conditions after ADM-012's email-match fix) — plan Section B step 5 + Execute-Agent Instruction E5 correctly add isNull(revokedAt) as a 5th condition, verified against the live 4-condition WHERE, not the plan's original stale 3-condition assumption; (3) staff.ts create-time supersede predicate (lines 254-263, currently 3 conditions) — plan Section C step 6 adds isNull(revokedAt) as a 4th; (4) the new list/revoke/resend routes' own 'is pending' predicates (GET /invites' filter, POST /revoke's compare-and-swap WHERE, POST /resend's read-then-write pair) — all independently spelled out with isNull(revokedAt) in the Public Contracts section, not derived from one shared helper function that a call site could forget to invoke. No site was missed in the plan's own accounting, and this pass independently found the same 4 sites by reading the code directly rather than trusting the plan's list. RESIDUAL NOTE (not a design flaw, an EXECUTE-time risk): because each site duplicates its own isNull(revokedAt) check rather than calling one shared predicate function, a future 5th liveness-check site added later (by some future phase) could forget the guard — this is the same duplication pattern the existing consumedAt/expiresAt checks already use (also duplicated across sites, not centralized), so it is consistent with the codebase's existing convention, not a new risk this plan introduces. AC4 is Known-Gap-banned specifically because this is the one invariant the team decided must be proven, not merely designed.

scenarios.scenario: Resend's send-before-commit ordering does not actually prevent a stranded invite if delivery fails — e.g. the rotation could commit before send confirms, or a partial failure could leave the row in an inconsistent state.
scenarios.rationale: The Public Contracts section for POST /invites/:id/resend explicitly sequences: compute fresh rawToken/tokenHash/expiresAt in memory -> await sendStaffInvite(email, rawToken) -> only on send success run the atomic UPDATE. If sendStaffInvite throws, the function returns a delivery-failure error WITHOUT ever calling the UPDATE, so the row's existing tokenHash/expiresAt are untouched and the old (still-pending, per step 3's read) link remains valid. This is the OPPOSITE ordering of invite-create's own fire-and-forget pattern (create commits first, sends after, tolerating send failure because there is no prior working link to lose) — the plan correctly identifies why resend cannot reuse that ordering and designs the inverse. One real, not-yet-closed gap: the plan's step 3 read and the eventual step 7 atomic UPDATE are two separate DB round-trips with the `await sendStaffInvite` in between — if the invite is revoked or consumed by a DIFFERENT admin/invitee in that window, the plan's own step 8 explicitly handles this ('no row returned -> 404'), closing the TOCTOU by the same atomic compare-and-swap WHERE the UPDATE already uses, not by the ordering alone. Ruled out: no code path exists in the design where a rotation commits without a confirmed send, or where a stale read silently wins a race.

scenarios.scenario: Revoke or resend can be used to smuggle a role/branch change via a request body — e.g. POST /invites/:id/resend with an alternate intendedRole in the body silently changing what the eventual accept will grant.
scenarios.rationale: The plan's Public Contracts for /invites/:id/resend states explicitly: 'ignore the request body entirely — no Zod schema parses role/branch from it; only :id is read from params.' This closes the vector STRUCTURALLY (no code path reads role/branch from the body at all), not merely by validating-and-rejecting a mismatched value — the stronger of the two possible designs, matching the same 'read stored values only, never body-supplied' discipline ADM-011's /consume handler already established for the analogous role/branch-smuggling scenario at accept time (confirmed by reading staff-invite.ts's /consume handler, which returns 'intendedRole'/'intendedBranchId' from the DB row via .returning(), never from req.body). Revoke's contract takes no body fields at all beyond the URL :id, so there is no body-smuggling surface on that route either. AC6 explicitly requires a test asserting a smuggled payload is ignored — this is a Known-Gap-adjacent but NOT Known-Gap-banned AC in the contract; still, the structural design (never parsing the field) is a materially stronger guarantee than a would-be validate-and-reject design.

scenarios.scenario: Part B: a super_admin demotes THEMSELVES (self-lockout of their own account).
scenarios.rationale: Self-demotion is structurally impossible: users.ts:63 rejects `req.params.id === req.adminSession!.userId` with 400 'Cannot modify own role' BEFORE the Zod body parse, for ANY target role including 'customer' — confirmed by direct read, this guard is not role-target-specific. Because a super_admin can never target their own id, no single self-action can reduce the super_admin count via self-demotion. Ruled out for the SPECIFIC self-lockout scenario.

scenarios.scenario: Part B: a super_admin demotes ANOTHER super_admin, or the last remaining super_admin, driving the super_admin count toward zero over multiple actions by different callers (community lockout).
scenarios.rationale: ACCEPTED RESIDUAL — NOT ruled out. Demoting a DIFFERENT super_admin is NOT blocked by any guard: the plan's Section H dimension finding explicitly acknowledges 'a super_admin demoting a DIFFERENT super_admin is an intentional admin decision the SPEC does not restrict,' and there is deliberately NO last-super_admin protection. A determined or careless sequence of actions by different callers (A demotes B, then a different account promotes nobody back) COULD in principle reduce the super_admin count toward zero. This is accepted, not structurally prevented, and matches the SPEC's explicit choice not to add last-super_admin protection — see the 'What this coverage does NOT prove' section of the plan's Validate Contract, which explicitly flags this has not been probed by vc-predict or a dedicated regression test. Preserved here as an accepted residual, separated from the self-demotion case (which IS ruled out, above).

scenarios.scenario: Demotion leaves dangling authority after role drop — e.g. an already-issued admin-web session cookie or mobile bearer token for the demoted user continues to grant staff/admin-level API access until it naturally expires, even though the DB row now says 'customer'.
scenarios.rationale: RULED OUT post-Fix-1 (this scenario was originally flagged NOT-ruled-out in the pre-fix design pack; Fix 1 / AC14 subsequently resolved it). Every admin/staff route in this codebase (requireAdmin, the inline super_admin checks, requireStaff on /api/staff/*) authorizes based on a FRESH read of the user's CURRENT role at request time — requireAdmin's getSession call resolves role from the live session/user lookup each request, and auth.ts's session block configures no cookieCache, so no stale role claim is baked into a long-lived token (confirmed by direct read of require-admin.ts). So the very next authenticated request a demoted user makes to any staff/admin-gated route correctly sees role='customer' and is rejected; the window is bounded by session-lookup freshness, not token lifetime. Fix 1 added an explicit design statement AND a proving regression test — AC14: demote a staff user mid-test, reuse their pre-demotion session on a guarded route, assert the very next request is rejected — so this is now proven, not merely assumed. No session revocation is required.

scenarios.scenario: The new GET /invites list route leaks tokenHash or another secret-adjacent field in its JSON response, enabling offline brute-force or replay if intercepted.
scenarios.rationale: The plan's Section D serializer design (AdminPendingStaffInvite interface) explicitly lists only id/email/intendedRole/intendedBranchId/intendedBranchName/invitedByName/invitedByEmail/createdAt/expiresAt — tokenHash is not in the shape, and the plan's own AC1 test explicitly asserts 'no tokenHash field present anywhere in the response,' matching the SPEC's explicit constraint ('tokenHash must never appear in any serialized response from the list route... it is a secret-adjacent column, same discipline ADM-011 already established'). This mirrors the existing serializeAdminStaffInvite precedent (create-response shape), which already excludes tokenHash — confirmed by direct read of serializers.ts.

scenarios.scenario: The list route or another new endpoint could be used as an enumeration oracle — e.g. probing a range of invite ids via revoke/resend's 404 response to infer which ids exist, or probing arbitrary emails.
scenarios.rationale: GET /invites is super_admin-gated (401/403 boundary), so only an already-privileged caller could enumerate it, and it already returns the full pending set directly — there is no incremental-guessing angle since the authorized caller sees everything at once. Revoke/resend's uuid-shape guard + atomic compare-and-swap both return an undifferentiated 404 for 'id doesn't exist' AND 'id exists but is not pending' — this collapses the oracle to a single bit (pending vs not-pending-or-nonexistent) and only for an already-super_admin-authenticated caller, which is not a meaningful new attack surface beyond what a super_admin already has full visibility into via GET /invites itself. The unauthenticated-facing /staff-invite/start route (pre-existing from ADM-011, not new in this plan) already has its own separately-analyzed rate-limiter and 404/410/200 status distinction, evaluated by ADM-011's own adversarial-validation.json — unaffected by ADM-013's changes to that route beyond adding a 3rd revoked-check OR-arm.

scenarios.scenario: A revoked or resent invite's OLD token, if somehow still cached client-side or replayed by a slow/retrying client, could still succeed if there is a race between the plan's read-then-write steps in resend, or between revoke's compare-and-swap and a concurrent /consume attempt.
scenarios.rationale: RULED OUT post-Fix-2 (the ORIGINAL pre-fix rationale here correctly identified that resend's WHERE keyed on pending-status ONLY — not on the specific token being rotated — so two back-to-back resends could each 'win' in sequence, the second silently killing the first's just-delivered link; Fix 2 closed exactly this). Revoke is a single atomic UPDATE ... WHERE consumed_at IS NULL AND revoked_at IS NULL AND expires_at > now() RETURNING id — the same compare-and-swap shape already proven safe for /consume's own atomic claim (ADM-011's adversarial-validation.json already ruled out a token-replay race using this exact pattern). Resend's rotating UPDATE is NOW keyed on the row's CURRENT tokenHash, captured at the same read that checks pending status (exact-token compare-and-swap) — a racing second resend whose captured hash is now stale (because the first resend already rotated it) matches ZERO rows and returns 404 with zero DB mutation, so it can no longer re-rotate and invalidate the first resend's just-delivered link. Proven by AC15's deterministic regression. ACCEPTED RESIDUAL (send-before-CAS design): because both a racing pair's pending-check reads happen before either's send/write, a genuine simultaneous double-resend can still DELIVER two emails, one of which points at a token that will never validate (one invalidated link) — a UX rough edge, not a security or data-integrity defect; the DB-level safety property (404 + zero mutation for the stale-token loser) holds unconditionally.
